title: From Blood and Ash vs Serpent & Dove: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Jennifer L. Armentrout's From Blood and Ash vs Shelby Mahurin's Serpent & Dove—which fantasy romance is better? Complete comparison.

book1.title: From Blood and Ash
book1.author: Jennifer L. Armentrout
book1.description: Chosen from birth to usher in a new era, Poppy's life has never been her own. The life of the Maiden is solitary. Never to be touched. Never to be looked upon. Never to be spoken to. Never to experience pleasure. Waiting for the day of her Ascension, she would rather be with the guards, fighting back the evil that took her family, than preparing to be found worthy by the gods. But the choice has never been hers. The entire kingdom's future rests on Poppy's shoulders, something she's not even quite sure she wants.

book2.title: Serpent & Dove
book2.author: Shelby Mahurin
book2.description: Two years ago, Louise le Blanc fled her coven and took shelter in the city of Cesarine, forsaking all magic and living off whatever she could steal. But when Lou is forced into a marriage with Reid Diggory, a witch-hunter who despises everything she is, she finds herself drawn into a world she thought she'd left behind forever. With danger lurking around every corner, Lou and Reid must learn to trust each other—or risk losing everything.

book1Strengths: Nearly DOUBLE the readers—285,000 ratings vs 145,000. From Blood and Ash became a massive phenomenon while Serpent & Dove stayed more niche. The popularity gap tells you which book captured hearts harder

The Hawke/Casteel identity reveal is one of romantasy's most devastating twists—he's not her protector, he's Casteel Da'Neer, Atlantian prince, the Dark One everyone fears, playing her from page one. That betrayal-into-love arc hits different
Higher rating (4.5 vs 4.1) shows stronger execution—FBAA has better pacing, tighter plotting, and more satisfying character arcs. The 0.4 star gap is significant when both have 100K+ ratings

Poppy's forbidden Maiden premise is DELICIOUS—every touch, every stolen moment, every act of rebellion matters when she's literally forbidden from being touched. The bodyguard watching forbidden fruit creates nuclear sexual tension

JLA's world-building goes deeper and stays consistent: Atlantians (immortal, fanged), Ascended (parasitic vampires), wolven bonds, Primal gods, the Joining ritual. By book 3-4 you're immersed in rich mythology that actually makes sense

The spice level is MUCH higher—explicit scenes, dirty talk, the legendary Joining scene in book 2. If you want heat with your fantasy, FBAA delivers consistently while Serpent & Dove keeps it more modest
Series is complete (6 books, ended 2024)—you can binge the entire saga without waiting. Serpent & Dove trilogy finished too, but FBAA's scope is way bigger
Poppy's snarky voice keeps it fun—even when she's the obedient Maiden, her internal monologue roasts the Ascended's hypocrisy and dreams of stabbing people. The humor balances heavy themes
book1Weaknesses: The first 200 pages are SLOW—Poppy's locked in castle doing Maiden duties while exposition dumps about the Ascended pile up. If you're not immediately hooked by forbidden romance tension, the pacing will frustrate you
At 619 pages it's a chunky commitment vs Serpent & Dove's 530. And chapters run LONG (some 30+ pages) with no natural breaks. Not a quick weekend read
Poppy makes infuriatingly naive choices even after learning the truth—trusts obvious villains, gets captured repeatedly, ignores screaming red flags. By book 3-4 you're yelling USE YOUR BRAIN

The series quality nosedives books 4-5—what started as Atlantians vs Ascended spirals into Primals, gods, time travel, reincarnation chaos. Many fans loved books 1-2 then felt betrayed by mythology overload

Power dynamics are sketchy—Hawke lies to Poppy for 400 pages, manipulates her emotionally, kidnaps her. The 'it's okay because he loved her all along' excuse doesn't sit right with everyone. Consent gets murky
More expensive ($14.99 vs $12.99) and cliffhanger endings FORCE you to buy sequels immediately. JLA knows how to make you spend money on the whole series
book2Strengths: The French-inspired Cesarine setting is GORGEOUS—cobblestone streets, chasseurs (witch-hunting holy warriors), cathedral magic, blood witches vs church. The witchhunt vibes are unique in romantasy where most go full medieval England

Lou le Blanc is hilarious—a thieving witch hiding among witch-hunters, sarcastic as hell, zero patience for Reid's religious uptightness. Her internal commentary roasting chasseur purity culture is chef's kiss

The forced marriage setup is deliciously awkward—Lou needs protection, Reid needs a wife to advance in the chasseurs, neither can stand each other. Watching a witch-hunter slowly fall for an actual witch is peak irony

The enemies-to-lovers burn is SLOWER and more earned than FBAA—Reid has to unlearn literal religious brainwashing that witches are evil. His crisis of faith as he falls for Lou feels psychologically real, not just instalust
Cheaper ($12.99 vs $14.99) and shorter (530 vs 619 pages) makes it more accessible for readers testing the waters on romantasy without massive commitment

The magic system has creative specificity—blood magic (Lou's grandmother's grimoire, sacrificial spells), chasseur blessing magic, Dame Blanche white witches. The magical taxonomy feels thought-through
Mahurin balances humor and darkness well—Lou's chaotic energy keeps it fun even when chasseurs are burning witches. The tone is lighter than FBAA's brooding intensity
The ending wraps more satisfyingly for book 1—you CAN stop here if you want. FBAA's cliffhanger demands you buy book 2 immediately
book2Weaknesses: The rating gap is brutal—4.1 vs 4.5 stars tells you FBAA executed better. That 0.4 difference with 100K+ ratings each means consistent reader disappointment with Serpent & Dove's pacing, plotting, or payoff
HALF the readership—145,000 vs 285,000. Serpent & Dove didn't capture mass appeal the way FBAA did. It's more niche, less phenomenon

Reid's witch-hunter fanaticism is HARD to swallow for 300+ pages—he literally believes witches are evil demons who deserve burning. His redemption arc requires MASSIVE suspension of disbelief that Lou would ever forgive his complicity in witch genocide

The slower burn frustrates readers wanting heat—FBAA delivers spice by midbook, Serpent & Dove makes you wait until near the end. If you want sexual tension that actually pays off, this tests patience

Books 2-3 (Blood & Honey, Gods & Monsters) get progressively WORSE—convoluted plotting, character assassination, unsatisfying ending. Many readers loved book 1 then rage-quit the trilogy. FBAA has quality issues too, but not this steep a decline

Some plot conveniences strain credibility—Lou hiding her witch identity in a city FULL of chasseurs for years? Reid not noticing magical tells? The forced marriage solving everything? It requires generous suspension of disbelief
Less steamy overall—if you want explicit romance, FBAA delivers way more heat. Serpent & Dove keeps it fade-to-black or vague for most of the book

book1: You are the most beautiful thing I've ever seen, Poppy.
You're an absolutely stunning, murderous little creature.
I will always find you. In this life or the next. There is no other option. There is nothing else.
You can run. I like to chase.
I'm yours. I'm so very yours.
From blood and ash, we will rise.
book2: A kiss for luck, then. And perhaps another after.
I would burn down the world for you.
Chaos and cunning. That's what we are.
You are my wife. For better or worse, you're mine.
Some things are worth the risk.
Magic always comes with a price.

book1: Experience one of fantasy romance's biggest recent hits
Hawke/Casteel is swoon-worthy with protective alpha energy
High steam level for readers wanting spice
Major plot twists that completely change everything
JLA's signature banter and sexual tension
Poppy's growth from sheltered Maiden to powerful heroine
book2: Get enemies-to-lovers with forced marriage trope
Good balance of humor, magic, and romance
Perfect if FBAA's intensity is too much

verdict.summary: From Blood and Ash dominates with 4.5 stars and 285,000 ratings vs Serpent & Dove's 4.1 and 145,000—nearly DOUBLE the readership and better execution. The Hawke/Casteel betrayal-into-love twist is more devastating, the forbidden Maiden premise creates better sexual tension, and JLA's mythology stays coherent longer (books 1-3 solid vs S&D's steep decline books 2-3). Serpent & Dove has a unique French witch-hunter setting and cheaper price, but can't match FBAA's phenomenon status.
verdict.recommendation: Read From Blood and Ash if you want the romantasy that captured 285,000 readers and doesn't let go. The Hawke identity reveal (he's Casteel Da'Neer, Atlantian prince, the Dark One) is one of the genre's most devastating twists. Poppy's forbidden Maiden status (can't be touched) makes every stolen moment nuclear. The world-building goes deep—Atlantians, Ascended parasites, wolven bonds, Primal gods, the Joining ritual. At 619 pages it's long but addictive, and the spice level is HIGH (explicit scenes, dirty talk, that legendary Joining scene book 2). Complete 6-book series means bingeable saga. Just know: books 4-5 jump the shark with mythology chaos, and Poppy makes frustrating naive choices. THEN read Serpent & Dove if you want French-inspired witch-hunter vibes and slower-burn enemies-to-lovers. Lou (thieving witch) forced to marry Reid (chasseur witch-hunter) creates delicious irony. The magic system (blood magic vs cathedral blessings) is creative, Lou's sarcasm is hilarious, and Reid's crisis of faith feels earned. At 530 pages and $12.99 it's cheaper and shorter. But be warned: 4.1 rating shows weaker execution, books 2-3 decline steeply, and Reid's witch-burning complicity is HARD to forgive. If you only read one, From Blood and Ash is the safer bet—double the readers, higher rated, more phenomenon than niche.
